planting.note: Divide clumps in spring or fall in dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; hairy spiderwort grows naturally in dry, rocky, open woodlands and rocky outcrops of the southeastern US.

directions: Plant in dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in partial shade to full sun; hairy spiderwort is a native southeastern spiderwort adapted to dry, rocky, open woodland conditions — more drought-tolerant and shade-tolerant than most tradescantias. The name reflects the long, conspicuous hairs on the stem (hirsuticaulis = hairy stem). The flowers are typically rose-pink to lavender-pink rather than the blue-violet of Ohio spiderwort, offering a different color palette. The characteristic three-petaled, ephemeral flowers open each morning and close by afternoon. As a relatively small, compact species growing in dry, rocky soils, it is useful in rock gardens and dry shade settings where larger, moisture-loving tradescantias would not thrive.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in moist to well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Asiatic dayflower self-sows prolifically and spreads easily in garden beds. Often treated as a weed, but its young leaves and stems are edible.

directions: Grows in moist to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Asiatic dayflower is a common garden weed from East Asia, now naturalized throughout North America in disturbed, moist, shaded habitats. While usually regarded as a pest, the plant has significant culinary and ornamental value — the bright blue, three-petaled flowers (two large blue petals and one tiny white petal) are among the most intensely sky-blue flowers of any common plant, and were historically used as a blue dye and pigment for Japanese woodblock printing (aizuri). Young leaves, stems, and flowers are edible raw in salads or cooked as a vegetable in East Asian cuisine. The flowers are also used as an edible garnish. An interesting plant to appreciate as an edible rather than simply eradicate as a weed.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in shallow water or consistently wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil at pond edges; lanceleaf pickerelweed grows in freshwater marshes, shallow ponds, and slow-moving streams throughout the southeastern Coastal Plain.

directions: Plant in shallow water (2–6 inches deep) or consistently wet, slightly acidic to neutral, organic-rich soil at pond margins; lanceleaf pickerelweed is a southeastern relative of the common pickerelweed (P. cordata), distinguishable by its narrower, lance-shaped leaves rather than the broad, heart-shaped leaves of P. cordata. The flower spikes bear the same attractive blue-violet flowers as common pickerelweed and bloom from spring through fall, attracting numerous bumblebees and native bees that are essential pollinators of the aquatic flower. The seeds are eaten by wood ducks and other waterfowl. In zone 9a, it performs as a reliable, warm-climate aquatic perennial at pond edges and in constructed wetlands, where it spreads by rhizomes to form naturalistic colonies.

planting.note: Direct sow on wet, sandy substrate or plant divisions in spring in shallow water or wet soil in partial shade to full sun; grassleaf arrowhead grows naturally in pond margins, slow streams, and other wet habitats with variable water levels.

directions: Plant in shallow water to consistently wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; grassleaf arrowhead is an aquatic native with unusual leaf variation — unlike the typical arrowhead-shaped leaves of most Sagittaria, this species produces narrow, grass-like leaves (particularly when submerged) that give it its common name. The plant adapts its leaf form to water depth: submerged leaves are very narrow and strap-like, emergent leaves may be broader and more typical of the genus. Small, three-petaled white flowers are produced on erect stems above the water. Provides food for waterfowl (the small corms are eaten by ducks) and habitat for small aquatic invertebrates. An adaptable native aquatic for constructed wetlands, bog gardens, and the shallow margins of ornamental ponds in zones 5–10.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in shallow water or wet, slightly acidic to neutral, organic-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; delta arrowhead grows naturally in the sluggish backwaters and river margins of the Mississippi River Delta and Gulf Coastal Plain wetlands.

directions: Plant in shallow water (0–8 inches deep) or consistently wet, slightly acidic to neutral, organic-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; delta arrowhead is a robust native aquatic of the lower Mississippi River basin and Gulf Coastal Plain river systems, producing broad, somewhat ovate to broadly arrow-shaped leaves and the characteristic three-petaled white flowers of arrowheads. The plant is valuable in slow-moving water and constructed wetlands, where its dense growth helps stabilize banks, provides fish habitat structure, and produces corms and seeds consumed by waterfowl. An important plant for biological water quality improvement (phytoremediation) in constructed wetlands processing agricultural runoff. In the ornamental pond or rain garden, it creates a bold, native aquatic presence with seasonal white flowers.
